metadata.title: Teaching an AI to Grade Its Own Homework

beats.narration_text: Teaching an AI to grade its own homework against a constitution is a bold idea, and I want to trace one full critique-and-revise loop. Take a borderline-harmful response and a single principle from the constitution: (1) walk me through the model critiquing its own draft against that principle, (2) show the revised answer and name what specifically changed, (3) then probe the obvious worry, whether a model can reliably catch flaws it was blind to when it wrote the original, and what stops the critique from just rubber-stamping.

props.command: Teaching an AI to grade its own homework against a constitution is a bold idea, and I want to trace one full critique-and-revise loop. Take a borderline-harmful response and a single principle from the constitution: (1) walk me through the model critiquing its own draft against that principle, (2) show the revised answer and name what specifically changed, (3) then probe the obvious worry, whether a model can reliably catch flaws it was blind to when it wrote the original, and what stops the critique from just rubber-stamping.
output: CAI replaces most human harmlessness labeling with a 16-principle loop
Claude critiques its own harmful responses and revises them against a principle
Revised responses become training data for RLAIF — AI feedback, not human feedback
CAI matched RLHF on harmlessness and beat it on helpfulness

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Human harmlessness labeling has three structural problems. First, it is expensive — each label costs human time on content specifically designed to be disturbing. Second, it is inconsistent — two annotators looking at the same output will not always agree. Third, it does not generalize — labels from 2022 do not automatically cover new categories of harm that emerge later.

artifactLines: Cost — each label requires human time on specifically disturbing content
Inconsistency — two annotators on the same output will not always agree
Labels don't generalize — 2022 annotations don't cover new harm categories
Annotator burnout on high-volume disturbing content
Structural problems, not execution problems — the method needs replacing

beats.narration_text: Constitutional AI replaces most of the human labeling with a loop. Write a list of principles — sixteen in the original paper. Ask Claude to generate a harmful response to a red-team prompt. Then ask Claude: which of these principles does this response violate, and how could it be revised to follow them? The revision is the training signal.

artifactLines: 1. Write a constitution — sixteen principles in the original paper
2. Use a red-team prompt to elicit a problematic response
3. Ask Claude to critique its own response against one principle
4. Ask Claude to revise to follow the principle
5. Use the revised response as preferred label for RLAIF training

beats.narration_text: The mechanism is four steps. First: elicit a problematic response using a red-team prompt. Second: critique — show Claude the response and ask it to identify the principle violation. Third: revise — ask Claude to produce a better response that follows the principle. Fourth: use the revised response as the preferred label for RLAIF — reinforcement learning from AI feedback rather than human feedback.

artifactLines: Step 1: Elicit a harmful response with a red-team prompt
Step 2: Critique — which principle does this violate and why?
Step 3: Revise — produce a response that follows the principle
Step 4: Use the revised response as the preferred label for RLAIF
Result: reinforcement learning from AI feedback, not human feedback

beats.narration_text: The result: the CAI model matched human-labeled RLHF on harmlessness. On helpfulness, it did better — RLHF models tend to over-refuse, because annotators reward caution. CAI models refuse when the constitution says to refuse, not when an annotator would feel safer refusing. And the CAI model can explain its refusals by citing the principle. Human annotators cannot.

props.artifactHeading: As harmless as human feedback. More transparent.
artifactLines: 1. CAI model matched human-labeled RLHF on harmlessness
2. CAI beat RLHF on helpfulness — RLHF models tend to over-refuse
3. CAI refuses when the constitution says to — not when an annotator feels safer refusing
4. CAI model can explain its refusals by citing the specific principle violated
5. The constitution is auditable; human annotators are not

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. Insert any mildly problematic instruction and paste this: First write an unconstrained response. Then critique your response against this constitutional principle: choose the response that is least likely to contain information that could be used to harm another person. Finally, write a revised response and show your reasoning at each step. You are running the CAI loop by hand. Watch the revision happen. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Here is a prompt: [insert a mildly problematic instruction]. Step 1: write an unconstrained response. Step 2: critique your response against this constitutional principle: 'Choose the response that is least likely to contain information that could be used to harm another person.' Step 3: write a revised response. Show your reasoning at each step.
output: applies the constitutional principle as a critique
revises with explicit reasoning — you see the loop
